entries.text: The river gives you up at the weir. You keep none of it in order: hands in your collar, gravel under your back, the sky white and near, water leaving you in coughs. A stone jizō watches from the bank, rice at its feet, nobody on the road who could have left it. Someone says, carry him up, then. Then nothing.
Straw. A low roof. A fire kept small. Something surfaces — a knot, a road in grey rain — and goes down again before you can put a hand to it. You have been three days in a room you have never seen.

The physician does not ask how you feel. He takes your wrist, then your jaw, then turns your hands over and looks at them longer than at anything else. He presses a thumb into the callus line across one palm and says nothing. There is a ledger open on his knee.
"Do you know the year?"
entries.speaker: Sōan

entries.text: "No."
"An'ei nine. Now you know it again. Drink this."
entries.speaker: Sōan

entries.text: Whatever the hands told him goes into the ledger, two lines, too far off to read. The door slides, and he closes the book on his thumb.
The man in the doorway is grey and dry and carries a book of his own. Two books in the room now, and only one of them open.
"The weir man. Sōan — can he work?"
entries.speaker: Genemon

entries.text: "Ask him."
entries.speaker: Sōan

entries.text: "Name, age, where from. In that order."
entries.speaker: Genemon

entries.text: Three answers, and you do not have one of them. The silence goes on long enough that the physician looks up.
"What name did I give? When they pulled me out."
"None. You gave none. Three days I have had nothing to call you, and neither have you."
entries.speaker: Sōan

entries.text: Nothing comes.
Genemon does not look surprised. He looks down and writes, and reads it back as he writes, aloud and once.
One man, name unknown. Taken at the weir. Fed, three days. Condition, poor; hands, good.
entries.speaker: Genemon

entries.text: "When he can stand, he rakes. The kitchen wants water before it wants anything."
entries.speaker: Genemon

entries.text: He goes. Sōan opens his ledger again, where his thumb kept the place.
You have started to keep count of your own strength — what the day has drawn out of you, and how far that is from Sōan's pallet.
The kura's count is open to you now: rice in, rice out, and the thin line between the store and a lean winter.
"My hands know a knot. I'll keep that."
entries.speaker: You

entries.text: You tie it in the cord of your own bedding: a carrier's hitch, fast and one-handed, twice. Sōan watches it both times. He does not write it down while you are looking.
entries.speaker: Sōan

entries.text: Perk unlocked — The Carrier's Hitch: fast, one-handed, holds wet. (+1 AGI / −1 INT)

So you put your hands to it. Rake what grain is still whole back toward the basket, a fistful at a time. We reckon a samurai house’s worth in koku — a year’s eating for one man — and every basket you save us is a measure we need not go begging to the lord to make good.
entries.speaker: Genemon

entries.text: You can stop now, when the day is done — set the work down, and let the body take back what it can.
Clear it without my standing over you, and you'll have earned your rice and a dry corner to sleep in. I'll make you no grander promise than that. In this house, none of us holds one.
entries.speaker: Genemon

entries.text: "...You don't shirk the work," Genemon says, eyeing the cleared boards and the filling basket. "The house has had its fill of hands that do. Earn your keep, and a place here is yours."
"So he can work," Genemon says, in the way another man says good morning.
"Still at it," Genemon says, passing the granary door without stopping.
"Dawn, and he is still raking," Genemon says to the dark, as if the dark had asked.

Morning at the board. The hands take their rice at the threshold; the steward's papers hold the table's far end — an estate's worth of paper on a kitchen table. Genemon calls you up from the step without looking up.
"Two hands took the lowland road this week. Wages drawn through the day they left, lines struck. The yard is short. The work is not."
entries.speaker: Genemon

entries.text: It is the first you have heard of any leaving.

"Terms. Work, sun to sun, at what the yard wants doing — hired by the day, counted in the book. Meals at the threshold, morning and evening. The woodshed corner and a mat. No coin: the house's coin is spoken for before it reaches the yard. Objections are heard now or kept. The paddies want you by full light."
entries.speaker: Genemon

entries.text: Nobody stops eating. Two hands lost was a morning's talk; one stray kept is not even that. The bowls go on.
"The board has heard it. That serves for witness."
entries.speaker: Genemon

entries.text: He opens the day-book to a page already half-filled with other men.
On the veranda above the step, Naoyuki has stopped — the second son, papers of his own under one arm. Passing, and made a witness like the rest of the room.
"The house is glad of —"
entries.speaker: Naoyuki

entries.text: The sentence stops. He stands with it a moment, as if he has read it back and found it not worth the ink.
"Two hands left. You were here. That is all it is."
entries.speaker: Naoyuki

entries.text: He goes on along the veranda. He looked at you once.
Then Genemon takes up the brush, and asks it plainly, at board volume. The room hears without listening.
"One thing before the ink. If a claim follows you here, I enter it against your board. Is anything owing on you?"
entries.speaker: Genemon

entries.text: "Not that I know."
entries.speaker: Nameless

entries.text: "Then the book says none claimed. It has lived with worse."
entries.speaker: Genemon

entries.text: The gate and gateyard are yours to work now — stores come and go here, and Yohei sets up his stall on market days.
The terraced home paddies open to you — the rice that feeds the house.
They give you the lean space between the woodpiles: a mat, a chipped bowl, a nail for the coat. It is not much, and it is not anyone else's.
You begin to mark the turning of the days, and the four seasons with them.
Rank ↑ — The day-hand 日雇
The estate's own state of repair is yours to tend now — spend the house's surplus to shore it up.
The rice you clear and grow is food, not only trade — a plain bowl of it fills a hollow belly and puts you back to work the faster.
The first rows he plants alone come up as straight as anyone's.
The paddy women no longer point out which rows are his; he has learned them.
Coin, at last, and few enough to count on one hand. What little the house pays in mon, you keep your own tally of.
At the gate the porters shift a strap for him without being asked, as they do among their own.
A way to track what your hands are learning takes shape — your Skills.
The woodlot edge opens to you — kindling and timber to cut, wild greens to gather along its margin.
Out past the last worked row the setts begin — tanuki, badger — and the nightly raids on the drying racks and the seed store.
Rank ↑ — The yard-hand 庭男
You could boil the wild greens into a hot meal — plain fare, but a warm meal is what closes wounds and mends a body after a fight.
First light. The broom stands against the gatepost where you left it. The hand who kept the yard's round quit for the lowlands; nobody has said whose it is now.
You take the broom. Nobody takes it back.

The round takes the morning: the gate swept, the forecourt raked, the water in, the wood up off the ground. Dusk round, then the kitchen threshold, the bowl. Genemon comes past with the day-book under his arm and does not slow.
"Rice from the new moon. One measure the day, dry, weighed at the kura door. Meals were terms for a man staying a week."
entries.speaker: Genemon

entries.text: He is through the door before you can set the bowl down.
A meal ends with the eating. Rice keeps.
The axe haft darkens where his grip has settled, and the stumps at the woodlot edge stand cut clean.
Under the silt, cut stone and a lift-board seized in its groove: a sluice-gate, made well and left to choke. Clearing it is days of honest digging, and nobody at the house knows it is there to clear.
The near hills give up bracken and mushrooms without a search; the basket comes home heavy before noon.
Bales pass the gate and none sit long; the storehouse floor shows bare wood where sacks once rotted.

Dawn comes grey through the kura door. The blood on the sill has dried black, and most of it is yours. Inside, the rice sits in its rows, untouched, exactly as it was put. Sōan has strapped your ribs so tight that breathing is a decision.
Kihei crouches at the sill a long time, reading the blood the way Genemon reads a column of figures. The trail goes west, into the trees, in drops that get no smaller.
"…Alive. Good."
entries.speaker: Kihei

entries.text: For a moment there is more in him than the verdict. He checks the broken bar instead.
Genemon stands in the doorway. Whatever he came to say, he writes something in the day-book first.
"Screen, one, torn. Bar, one, broken. Grain — none lost. Hand, one. Standing."
entries.speaker: Genemon

entries.text: None lost. You think of the gnawed seed-bale at the back wall, and the count you made twice by lantern, and you do not correct him. Not yet.
"The bar was old. It goes against the house, not your wage."
entries.speaker: Genemon

entries.text: Kihei stands, takes the boar-spear off the sill, and puts it back in your hands, butt first.
"You didn't win. The house lost nothing. Keep the watch."
entries.speaker: Kihei

entries.text: By evening the padded coat is folded at the woodshed step, the torn shoulder closed with stitches smaller than the tear deserved. O-Hisa is still three steps from the kitchen when you come round the corner, caught.
"It tore clean, at least. If the ribs knit as straight — well."
entries.speaker: O-Hisa

entries.text: She is back inside before you find anything to say to that.
"It bled going west. The trail is fresh."
entries.speaker: Nameless

entries.text: "And you can chase it exactly as far as I can throw you. Sit down. It is winter's business now, not yours."
entries.speaker: Sōan

entries.text: The drill yard opens to you — a place to train, and foes that must be met.
You begin to keep a bestiary — a field-guide of the beasts of the estate, filling in as you face each one.

Standing in the drill yard, you begin to grasp how a house like this is truly weighed — not by its rice alone, but by its arms, its lands and coin, its standing before the lord, and the worth of its name. That reckoning sits far above a gate-watch. For now, you only see that it is there.
The storehouse is opened to you: the iron-strapped door, the good lock, the grain-watch's stool beside it. What is counted in and out is the house's whole year.
The shallows above and below the weir, reeds past the waist. River rats gnaw the screens the house leases from Matsuzō, and every screen lost is coin owed across the water.
Rank ↑ — The grain-watch 蔵番
A lean corner of the woodshed is kept back for you now — a straw mat, a bowl, a nail for your coat, a place to lay your head. "A place here is yours," the old man said. Small, but yours to keep.
You bring down the river rats. ✓ (HP 58→8)
The snow quits the valley floor first — gray at the edges, then standing water in the wheel-ruts. The ridge keeps its white and means to keep it.
The river runs black and narrow between its shelves of ice. Past the rope, the old garden's snow lies whole: a winter's worth, and not one track across it.
The light stretches. Work that used to end at the woodshed door now ends past it, and the cold stays on, but it has stopped arguing.
Small on the road below, a man comes up from the village with new straw rope coiled over his shoulder, for the gates. The year is going out. The New Year comes up the valley behind him.

Matsuzō comes up from the river at the pace of a man nothing has hurried in sixty years. Genemon meets him in the forecourt with the day-book already open — which is how you learn the house knew it was short before the knock.
"The weir lease. Due today: rice, one koku. In the store, against it: seven to. The house is short a third, and I will not count it smaller than it is."
entries.speaker: Genemon

entries.text: "Screens took a beating in the spring water. Rats after. Hard year on everything downstream."
entries.speaker: Matsuzō

entries.text: He does not say: on you too. He looks at the gate's paint, and at you, and back toward the river, the way he looks at everything — weather that will pass over his weir eventually.
"Three to owing. I ask the season's grace on it. The house asks."
entries.speaker: Genemon

entries.text: Genemon bows. Not deeply — the exact depth a Kurosawa steward has never before had to measure for a weir-keeper, measured now, got right the first time because he will not let it be seen twice.
"The river can wait a season. The screens can't — rats are in them now. Send your man down while the year turns and we'll call it even weight by winter."
entries.speaker: Matsuzō

entries.text: Nobody asks you. A line in the day-book, a nod toward the water, and a season of your evenings is settled between two old men. Three to of rice. It is the first time you have been worth a number. The number is small.

At the gate the old man stops, level with you, and looks at you the way he looked at the paint. He goes back down the way he came, having made nothing of it. He has been making nothing of it for a year.
You bring down the river rats. ✓ (HP 58→17)
You bring down the river rats. ✓ (HP 58→12 · +1 beast sinew)
"Quiet in the kura tonight," Kihei lets drop, cleaning his blade by lamplight.
You bring down the tanuki. ✓ (HP 58→16 · +1 beast sinew)
You bring down the tanuki. ✓ (HP 58→15)
"The monkeys have learned his shape," Kihei is heard to say, almost pleased.
"The fields don't miss him while he drills," Genemon notes, half to himself.
"Watch or no watch, his rows never fell behind," Genemon says over the evening rice.
"The palisade wood is his cutting," Kihei says, striking a post with the flat of his hand.
Ice lets go of the shaded bank a plate at a time, each one turning once in the current and gone.
The ground gives underfoot where all winter it rang. The well-path grows a margin of mud, and the mud holds each footprint till noon.
Up the woodlot, the bark of the young trees is chewed to the pale — deer, down off the ridge in the dark and gone again by light. Whatever they came for, the ridge no longer had it.
Last, and small under all that water-light, the villagers walk their field-bunds, looking at what the thaw left them. Spring is on the valley, and everything from here on is work.
The river rats overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 58→1) You drop 24 mon in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
The river rats overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 58→1) You drop 20 mon in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
The river rats overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 58→1) You drop 16 mon in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
The river rats overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 58→1) You drop 13 mon in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
The river rats overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 58→1) You drop 10 mon in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
You bring down the river rats. ✓ (HP 58→6)
You repair the worn carrying-pole. (−5 wood, −6 mon)
The river rats overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 58→1) You drop 7 mon in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
The river rats overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 58→1) You drop 6 mon in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
The river rats overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 58→1) You drop 5 mon in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
The river rats overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 58→1) You drop 4 mon in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
You bring down the river rats. ✓ (HP 58→12)
The river rats overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 58→1) You drop 5 mon in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
The river rats overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 58→1) You drop 5 mon in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
The green closes ranks. What was rows in the paddies is one surface now, and when the wind crosses it, the whole valley floor moves together.
The river drops slow over its gravel, clear and warm in the shallows by noon.
In the grove, the new bamboo has gone up taller than a man in a fortnight — pale among the old canes, then not.
People show late in this weather, and low: hats in the deep green, moving slow along the rows. Summer has the valley now.
The river rats overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 58→1) You drop 5 mon in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
The river rats overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 58→1) You drop 4 mon in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.

Out of the reeds, a bundle: river-heavy, the cloth gone the color of the bank, tied off in a knot your hands undo without being told. Inside, wrapped twice against the wet, a single paper — the ink run to gray, not one word of it left.
The river rats overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 58→1) You drop 3 mon in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
The river rats overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 58→1) You drop 3 mon in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
You repair the worn carrying-pole. (−5 wood, −6 mon)
The river rats overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 58→1) You drop 1 mon in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
The river rats overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 58→1) You drop 1 mon in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
The river rats overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 58→1) You drop 1 mon in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
You bring down the river rats. ✓ (HP 58→14)
Your body has hardened with the fighting. Combat level 2.
The badger overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 66→1) You drop 1 mon in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
You bring down the badger. ✓ (HP 66→1)
The badger overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 66→1) You drop 1 mon in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
You bring down the badger. ✓ (HP 66→2 · +1 beast sinew)
The heat loosens its grip at the edges first: a haze on the river that burns off later each morning, and at dusk the cicadas trade their noon voice for the thin evening one.
The light comes in slantwise now and finds the dust hanging in the forecourt air. The ridge stands closer in it, the way it does before weather.
On the hill above the village, small figures move among the stones with sickles and water-buckets, tidying — one household first, then by week's end all of them.
Smoke stands over the village at dusk, more fires than there are suppers. Bon is here, and the valley is making itself ready to be visited.
The badger overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 66→1) You drop 4 mon and 1 of your spoils in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
The badger overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 66→1) You drop 3 mon in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
The badger overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 66→1) You drop 2 mon in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
You repair the worn carrying-pole. (−5 wood, −6 mon)
The badger overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 66→1) You drop 1 mon in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
The badger overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 66→1) You drop 1 mon in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
At the wall's foot the spade finds the sett's main gallery: old work, wider than the badger needs, going down under the stones and not coming up on this side. The digging stops at the stones.
You bring down the badger. ✓ (HP 66→3)
The badger overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 66→1) You drop 1 mon in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
The badger overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 66→1) You drop 1 mon in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
The badger overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 66→1) You drop 1 mon in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
You bring down the badger. ✓ (HP 66→18 · +1 beast sinew)
You bring down the badger. ✓ (HP 66→2)
The badger overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 66→1) You drop 2 mon and 1 of your spoils in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
The badger overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 66→1) You drop 2 mon in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
Loads cross the forecourt all morning: trays, lamp-oil, cut flowers, a bundle of incense sticks O-Yae carries two-handed like a caught bird. Everyone is given something.
The third load goes past before you ask it.
"What do I carry?"
"Not this. If a year from now — well. Not this."
entries.speaker: O-Hisa

entries.text: The house files toward the alcove corridor. The forecourt is yours all day. For once, nobody crosses it.
At dusk, from the weir path: O-Ume sets her lantern on the water and talks it out past the reeds, the way you'd see a guest to the road.
At the gate, the monk Iori has his lodging and his one small fire.
"Sit. Two bowls, one mouth. I'll be gone before you learn my face."
entries.speaker: Iori

entries.text: Through the corridor screen, past shoulders: a small tray set out for somebody, and straw sandals beside it, toes toward the gate.
Morning. The trays are cleared, the lanterns down, the monk gone north. The sandals are new.
The river carries the first leaves down from the ridge, one and two at a time, red before anything on the valley floor has thought of turning.
The mornings come in cold enough to name. Mist stands knee-deep in the paddies, and the heavy heads of rice bow through it, all leaning the same way.
The road empties back out over the pass. The valley keeps what belongs to it and lets the rest go.

From the village at dusk, the sound of whetstones — one, then several, no hurry in any of them. Bon has gone home. It is autumn, and the valley's whole year is standing out in the fields, waiting to be carried in.
The badger overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 66→1) You drop 3 mon in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
You repair the worn carrying-pole. (−5 wood, −6 mon)
You bring down the badger. ✓ (HP 66→9)
Your body has hardened with the fighting. Combat level 3.

You have become what the house needed at its gate — a hand it can trust with an edge to it. Kihei watches you drill at first light and almost smiles. "There's a soldier in you under the farmhand. We'll find the rest of it yet." Past the gate the valley road climbs out of sight — toward the house's standing, its rivals, and the name you still cannot reach. That climb is for the days to come.

That night the dream comes again, and stays longer. The grey rain, the road — and now a load on your back, roped with the porter's knot your hands already knew. Water rising over the planks of a low bridge. A woman's voice behind you, calling a name through the flood — your name, you are almost sure, though the water closes over it before you can catch its shape.
The badger overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 74→1) You drop 2 mon in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
The badger overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 74→1) You drop 1 mon in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
The badger overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 74→1) You drop 1 mon in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
You bring down the badger. ✓ (HP 74→10)
You bring down the badger. ✓ (HP 74→2 · +1 beast sinew)
The badger overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 74→1) You drop 2 mon and 1 of your spoils in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
The badger overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 74→1) You drop 1 mon in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
The badger overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 74→1) You drop 1 mon in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
The badger overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 74→1) You drop 2 mon in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
The badger overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 74→1) You drop 2 mon in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.

Autumn thins to its last day, and the board comes out at an hour it does not usually keep. The whole house comes with it: the family by the book, the hands by the door, and you in the corner — counted as part of the room, and not asked into it.

Genemon reads the year against the house. The harvest in and weighed, the lease, the winter's wood — and then the one thing the house owes the land, that must be answered before the season is allowed to turn.
Nobody reads the sum aloud. It arrives the way weather arrives: the room goes still around it, a breath drawn and not let go, every eye on the book and none on one another.

Chiyo looks once, and briefly, at a screen that will not be in its room by spring. That is all that is shown of it, and none of it is explained. You are learning the house's true size by what it will let itself be seen without.
The book closes. The house has answered for the land; its autumn can end. You are still in the corner. Nobody tells you the season is over. It is.
The nengu is reckoned: the year measured against the house, the shortfall named plainly and let stand. No one at the board says the figure twice.

You bring down the badger. ✓ (HP 90→11)
The badger overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 90→1) You drop 2 mon in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
You bring down the badger. ✓ (HP 90→41)
The badger overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 90→1) You drop 2 mon in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
You bring down the badger. ✓ (HP 90→19 · +1 beast sinew)
You repair the worn carrying-pole. (−5 wood, −6 mon)
You bring down the badger. ✓ (HP 90→11)
You bring down the badger. ✓ (HP 90→10 · +1 beast sinew)
You bring down the badger. ✓ (HP 90→34 · +1 beast sinew)
You bring down the badger. ✓ (HP 90→6)
You bring down the badger. ✓ (HP 90→28 · +1 beast sinew)
You bring down the badger. ✓ (HP 90→14 · +1 beast sinew)
Your body has hardened with the fighting. Combat level 6.

Rats in the store, where Kihei said. You learn the sound grain makes when something small is stealing it — a dry whisper under the floor, gone the moment you stop walking. The spear-butt does the rest. In the morning you sweep up what they spoiled and say what you took. The number is never nothing.
The marten overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 14→1) You drop 5 mon and 2 of your spoils in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
You wake on Sōan's mat with daylight standing in the wrong place. Days gone, and the wages with them. He asks one question, writes two lines, and closes the ledger when you turn your head.

Rats in the store, where Kihei said. You learn the sound grain makes when something small is stealing it — a dry whisper under the floor, gone the moment you stop walking. The spear-butt does the rest. In the morning you sweep up what they spoiled and say what you took. The number is never nothing.
The marten overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 47→1) You drop 4 mon and 1 of your spoils in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
You wake on Sōan's mat with daylight standing in the wrong place. Days gone, and the wages with them. He asks one question, writes two lines, and closes the ledger when you turn your head.

On the dark of the moon a lantern crosses the far edge of the yard — hooded low, held steady, going upstream. By the time the round brings you back the yard is empty, and in the morning no one has missed a lantern. It is not your watch's business. You watch for it anyway.

Rats in the store, where Kihei said. You learn the sound grain makes when something small is stealing it — a dry whisper under the floor, gone the moment you stop walking. The spear-butt does the rest. In the morning you sweep up what they spoiled and say what you took. The number is never nothing.
The marten overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 28→1) You drop 3 mon and 1 of your spoils in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
You wake on Sōan's mat with daylight standing in the wrong place. Days gone, and the wages with them. He asks one question, writes two lines, and closes the ledger when you turn your head.

Rats in the store, where Kihei said. You learn the sound grain makes when something small is stealing it — a dry whisper under the floor, gone the moment you stop walking. The spear-butt does the rest. In the morning you sweep up what they spoiled and say what you took. The number is never nothing.
The marten overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 46→1) You drop 2 mon and 1 of your spoils in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
You wake on Sōan's mat with daylight standing in the wrong place. Days gone, and the wages with them. He asks one question, writes two lines, and closes the ledger when you turn your head.

Rats in the store, where Kihei said. You learn the sound grain makes when something small is stealing it — a dry whisper under the floor, gone the moment you stop walking. The spear-butt does the rest. In the morning you sweep up what they spoiled and say what you took. The number is never nothing.

A marten, quick as spilled water, dead by more luck than spear-work. Behind the back bales, where it hunted, you find what the rats had all along — a seed-rice bale gnawed open at the seam, half gone into the walls. You make the count alone, by lantern, twice. It does not improve the second time. You bar the door on it and tell no one yet.
At the kura door the lantern finds two eyes at the height of your waist, and the dark around them is the wrong shape for a dog. The bar is behind you. So is the rice.
The night the wolf came you stood between it and the stores, and were still there at dawn — bleeding, ribs cracked, the rice untouched. Not won. Survived.
Sōan strapped the ribs for walking. Not for work. It is thirty paces from the sickroom door to the board. You count them.

Past the kitchen passage a screen stands open on a corridor you have never been sent down. An altar set into the passage wall; rites laid out for somebody; a pair of straw sandals, new this year, facing away from the house. You look a breath too long. The screen slides shut from inside. Nobody says anything to you about it, then or after.
Genemon is there, the day-book open, the morning's entries not yet dry. He looks up, and waits, and does not help you begin.
"Rats were in the store. Three nights, while I lay. A quarter-sack gone. Two more opened at the seam."
"The watch was mine."
Genemon writes before he answers. It is what he does with surprise, though nothing in his face has admitted to any.
"Rice, one quarter-sack, to vermin. Two sacks to resew — O-Hisa has thread. Entered under the store."
entries.speaker: Genemon

entries.text: "Not under your name. No wage ran for the nights you lay. No watch stood — the gate cannot leave the gate. The account is even."
entries.speaker: Genemon

entries.text: "It isn't."
The pen stops above the line a moment longer than the line needs. Then it moves on, and he says nothing, and writes nothing further.
At the kitchen threshold O-Hisa has stopped with a bowl in her hands, as if there were something to say to somebody. She sets it down where you will pass, and goes in.
From the board, the woodshed is one way and the old stable court is the other. Your ribs are for the woodshed. You go the other way.

Stalls for twenty horses run down the long side of the court, and the house owns one mule. Kihei is at the rack, running a whetstone down a spear-head that has no war to go to. Two practice staves lean beside him. One is worn grey at the grip. The other is oiled, and looks as if it has never been anything else.
"Teach me."
That was the easy half. The question costs more than the walk did.
"Will you?"
Kihei sets down the whetstone. He looks at the strapping, at the way you keep your left side back, at the scars that were old before the river. He has looked at them before. He has never once asked.
"...You walked here on cracked ribs to ask for more of what cracked them."
entries.speaker: Kihei

entries.text: It is not a question, so you do not answer it.
"Terms, then. Once. Talent is a story the lucky tell. You are not lucky. So you will work."
entries.speaker: Kihei

entries.text: He takes the oiled staff from the rack and holds it out. He does not say how long it has stood there, and you do not ask.
"The house has one man who can stand a watch, and he is grey. Dawn, before the others. Every day the ribs allow, and some they don't."
entries.speaker: Kihei

entries.text: On the wall above the empty stalls a boy sits very still, the way boys sit when they believe stillness is the same as not being there.
"One thing before the staff. Why."
entries.speaker: Kihei

entries.text: "It will come back."
entries.speaker: Nameless

entries.text: "It will. Or its like. Dawn, then."
entries.speaker: Kihei

entries.text: You learn to read the wear on an edge — a blade dulls and chips with use, and a dull edge bites less. Mind your weapon's condition now.
The woodlot smithy is yours to use — strip what the carcasses give up, forge a real edge, keep it repaired, and take up whichever weapon suits the watch.
The omoya — the main house — is opened to you. Its shuttered rooms, closed since the lean years, are aired and swept; you walk floors the family walks.
The old stable court, swept bare and given a new use. Kihei's ground now, and yours to enter: things happen here in his order or not at all.
Rank ↑ — The pupil 弟子
The porters have stopped counting his loads against their own.
When the kura needs moving in a hurry, it is his name the yard calls first.
New porters are told to watch how he stacks; nobody remembers deciding that.
The house learns he keeps coin of his own, strung and counted, spent on nothing foolish.
The drillmaster teaches you to set your stance before a foe — press the attack and take more in return, or guard and give up some bite. The call is yours, fight by fight.
You begin to keep a book of undertakings — the tasks and errands the house sets you, and the ones you take on yourself. A place to see what still stands unfinished.
In passing you glimpse it — a family altar set into a corridor, the rites kept in a passage never meant to hold them. You are not asked to stop.
The old orchard gone wild: bramble to the shoulder, windfall rotting underfoot, the dogs denned in the hollow. Kihei calls it the dogs' yard, without irony.
You mount your worn carrying-pole on the wall of your corner — the weapon you carry, and proof of the road you have walked. A servant with a place, and a token of it on the wall.
Rank ↑ — The accused 咎人
The house pays you in coin now — a fixed measure for each day worked. It waits at the board until you go and take it up.
A hand shakes you awake in the dark of the woodshed. Rokusuke, with a lantern, looking mostly at the door.
"Board room. The whole house is called. You as well. I only fetch."
entries.speaker: Rokusuke

entries.text: Lamps at the board at the wrong hour. The household stands in two rows — the hands by the door, the house by the book — and between the rows there is more floor than the room usually has. You are shown to the middle of it. Nobody arranged that.

"The alcove chest stands open. One item short against the book. A packet — cloth, bound with cord, a hand's length — entered as lamp-oil for the shrine. Counted at the evening rice. Gone since. The house will be counted before it sleeps."
entries.speaker: Genemon

entries.text: Naoyuki stands at his father's place at the board. He does not look at you until he does.
"If the house must do this, then it does it properly — in order of service, eldest hands first, so that no one can say—"
entries.speaker: Naoyuki

entries.text: "No. The weir man. Him first."
entries.speaker: Naoyuki

entries.text: Nobody objects. Some of the hands look at you; more look at the floor. It is the order the room was already thinking in.
"Say nothing. Stand for the count."
entries.speaker: Nameless

entries.text: "Asked before the whole house, and he offers— note it down, steward. He offers nothing. Note that."
entries.speaker: Naoyuki

entries.text: "The kura tally. Who kept it today?"
entries.speaker: Genemon

entries.text: Rokusuke has been standing where the lantern light mostly isn't. He comes forward the way a man wades cold water.

"Eleven loads in by dark, steward. His mark against each. Last mark at lamp-lighting, and the rice was long cleared by then. He never left the kura side of the yard. That's what the board says. The board's there to look at."
entries.speaker: Rokusuke

entries.text: Genemon sets the tally board beside the day-book and reads one against the other. The room watches him do arithmetic.
"The packet was counted at the evening rice, in the inner house. From mid-day to lamp-lighting this man was under grain. The book holds no gap with him in it."
entries.speaker: Genemon

entries.text: "Then it is someone of the house."
entries.speaker: Naoyuki

entries.text: The door to the inner corridor slides. Toku stands in it, in her night-clothes, her hair down — a woman nobody sent for. She looks at no one in particular, and at the book last.
"It went up at the new moon."
entries.speaker: Toku

entries.text: Nobody asks what went up. Nobody asks where. Genemon dips his brush, enters one line, and closes the day-book.
"The count is answered. The house is not short. Take the lamps."
entries.speaker: Genemon

entries.text: The rows break up around you. Naoyuki passes you last.
"You were counted. That is all it was."
entries.speaker: Naoyuki

entries.text: At the morning board nobody speaks of the night. The hands keep a little more room around you than the work needs — for a day, then two — and then it is spent, needing nothing from you to end.
"From today the book carries you at a day-wage, in mon, paid against each day worked. It was due at the season's turn. It is the season's turn."
entries.speaker: Genemon

entries.text: At the paddy Rokusuke works the row over from yours, as he did before, no nearer. You thank him for the tally.
"Kept it same as any day. It would have read the same if it hanged you."
entries.speaker: Rokusuke

entries.text: He bends back to the row.
That evening the wage enters the day-book: so many mon, and against the entry, where a name would go, a mark. It is still not a name.
The first deep-hill baskets come back full, and no one mentions the danger line twice.
He comes back from past the charcoal-burners' marker so often the kitchen stops waiting up.
Whatever else he is now, the paddies get his mornings first, and Genemon sees that they do.
You lay a quilted futon over the straw in your corner. The nights come easier now.
There is bedding in the north corner, bought with his own coin, and the maids sweep around it now.
Morning, the forecourt. Genemon's window stands open, the day-book out, and beside it a purse the size of a fist, dark with handling.
"Salt, one shō. Waxed thread, two hanks. Lamp oil, one masu. Yohei sets his boards up at the gate by mid-morning."
entries.speaker: Genemon

entries.text: "Two hands short since spring, and I cannot leave the book on a market morning. So."
entries.speaker: Genemon

entries.text: He counts the coins into your palm one at a time, watching each one land.
"Forty mon. Count them back."
entries.speaker: Genemon

entries.text: "Forty."
He watches the whole count. When it comes out even he does not nod. He enters something in the day-book, unasked.
"His prices are his own. The house's figures are mine. Go."
entries.speaker: Genemon

entries.text: Market morning at the gate. Yohei's boards are up and the gateyard has people in it for once — baskets, a mule, talk. The woman ahead of you pulls her basket an inch closer when you stop beside her. The talk is rain over the pass and the price of salt, and it thins a little when you step to the boards, and it is not about you either.

"The house's hand, and carrying the house's purse — there's news by itself. Rain over the pass, the dye-man's held at the ford a week, so waxed thread is scarce, so the thread is eleven. Salt fifteen, the oil fourteen — fair as water, those two. Eleven for the thread, then?"
entries.speaker: Yohei

entries.text: "Nine. Count it with me."
entries.speaker: Nameless

entries.text: "Ha — the steward's figure, out of the steward's own mouth. Nine, nine, take it. Twelve markets a year, I'd rather be the stall you count at than the one you walk past."
entries.speaker: Yohei

entries.text: The estate's workshops wake again — a smith's forge, a joiner's bench — the crafts of a house that makes as well as grows.
A second granary, the itakura, is raised board by board — the house’s stores have outgrown the old kura at last.
Rank ↑ — The trusted hand 用人
"The store-room count is early this month," Chiyo says, not yet naming why.
"The ledgers agree tonight," Chiyo says, which is her whole praise.
You sell 24 rice to the pedlar at 5 mon the measure. (+1 monme 40 mon)
"His purse holds steady," Chiyo lets fall, "which is more than this house has managed."

You mend the leased weir screens slat by slat — green bamboo split with the grain, lashed wet to tighten as it dries. The rats' road is closed; the lease's damage line stays empty this season. (U1 · Stabilising)
"He put his own coin into the kura-works," Chiyo tells Genemon, twice, to be sure he heard.

The board, past supper, past the year's tax. The household's noises are done for the day. Genemon has the day-book open under the lamp and a fresh page ruled, and he does not look up until the last column of the old one is footed.
"Stand where I can see you. This is quick."
entries.speaker: Genemon

entries.text: "A full year and more, this book has said 'one man, name unknown.' Every entry, the same six strokes spent on a man the whole yard knows on sight. Paper is not free. Neither is my evening."
entries.speaker: Genemon

entries.text: He turns back through the pages — a winter, a wolf, a harvest — to the first entry, and reads it aloud, once. One man, name unknown. Then he dips the brush, and holds it over the page a breath longer than any entry needs.
"Gonbei. Every hired man this house has kept has carried it before you. The house's name for you, then. Earn a better."
entries.speaker: Genemon

entries.text: He writes it small and square — the hand of a man who has written all his life and never once for pleasure — and the thing is done. One man, Gonbei, sound.
"It will do."
entries.speaker: Nameless

entries.text: "So it will. Take what is left in the pot as you pass the kitchen. It is paid for."
entries.speaker: Genemon

entries.text: The lord opens his study — the shoin, the writing-room where the house's business is truly done — to you. Few servants ever cross that threshold.
Green dark and creaking, close behind the waste ground. The monkey troop raids the rows from here and is up the stems before you have turned around.
Rank ↑ — The named hand 名代

You stood in the room while the year was measured against the house — the reckoning read out, the gap named plainly and once, and nothing asked of you but to be there for it. A full year, lived through.

The woodshed, late. On the doorstep, folded under a stone against the wind: his straw coat, mended at the shoulder in small even stitches that no one will own to. He carries it in, lies down on the mat, and for once sleeps at once.
— a road, climbing. Rain on it.

The weight across his shoulders is right. Two loads and the pole, and his feet know the next stone without being asked. Below the road, water — he cannot see it, but it is there the way a wall is there in a dark room.
Behind him on the climb, somebody calls a name. Short, worn smooth, thrown easy — a name that has landed a thousand times and expects to land now.
He turns to answer.
The mountain moves.
After that there is only the water, and the road going away upward, and the voice still calling — smaller, and smaller. He knows the name is his. He cannot hear it.
He wakes in the woodshed with his mouth open around a word that is not there. Rain, real rain, small on the roof. The coat is where he left it.
"There was a road."
entries.speaker: Gonbei

entries.text: Nobody answers. It is a long while before he sleeps again, and the dream, that night, does not come back.
Entered: rice from the new paddies, credited to the house's own account. The old books carried this line once; it is carried again.
"The fields no longer wait on my orders," Munemasa says from the veranda, "they wait on his."
"Even the woodlot answers to him," Kihei says to Genemon, who does not disagree.
On the village road the house's name is said in the low voice kept for endings. Nobody wishes it ill; the valley is only deciding, early, what its fields will come to. (+2 koku)
"There is rice against winter," Munemasa says, slowly, as if the words were new to him.
Entered: the granary takes in more than it pays out. A full storehouse needs no remark — this one has earned it all the same.
You sell 24 rice to the pedlar at 5 mon the measure. (+1 monme 40 mon)
Entered: rice sold at market, the coin set down in the house books — owed to no man. The steward has waited some years to write that word.
You sell 24 rice to the pedlar at 5 mon the measure. (+1 monme 40 mon)
You sell 20 rice to the pedlar at 5 mon the measure. (+1 monme 20 mon)
On the village road the house's name is said in the low voice kept for endings. Nobody wishes it ill; the valley is only deciding, early, what its fields will come to. (+8 koku)
You sell 30 rice to the pedlar at 4 mon the measure. (+1 monme 40 mon)
You sell 27 rice to the pedlar at 4 mon the measure. (+1 monme 28 mon)
You sell 20 rice to the pedlar at 4 mon the measure. (+1 monme)

Autumn thins to its last day, and the board comes out at an hour it does not usually keep. The whole house comes with it: the family by the book, the hands by the door, and you in the corner — counted as part of the room, and not asked into it.

Genemon reads the year against the house. The harvest in and weighed, the lease, the winter's wood — and then the one thing the house owes the land, that must be answered before the season is allowed to turn.
Nobody reads the sum aloud. It arrives the way weather arrives: the room goes still around it, a breath drawn and not let go, every eye on the book and none on one another.

Chiyo looks once, and briefly, at a screen that will not be in its room by spring. That is all that is shown of it, and none of it is explained. You are learning the house's true size by what it will let itself be seen without.
The book closes. The house has answered for the land; its autumn can end. You are still in the corner. Nobody tells you the season is over. It is.
The nengu is reckoned: the year measured against the house, the shortfall named plainly and let stand. No one at the board says the figure twice.
On the village road the house's name is said in the low voice kept for endings. Nobody wishes it ill; the valley is only deciding, early, what its fields will come to. (+8 koku)
You sell 24 rice to the pedlar at 5 mon the measure. (+1 monme 40 mon)
You sell 24 rice to the pedlar at 5 mon the measure. (+1 monme 40 mon)
You sell 20 rice to the pedlar at 5 mon the measure. (+1 monme 20 mon)
The house's word is worth less down the valley than it was: at the stall its name still buys, but the amounts are counted slowly. (+9 koku)
The dens stand empty and the wild rows are cut back — sound fruit trees found under the choke. The day-book opens a line it has not carried in years: orchard. (U2 · Recovering)
You sell 24 rice to the pedlar at 5 mon the measure. (+1 monme 40 mon)
You sell 24 rice to the pedlar at 5 mon the measure. (+1 monme 40 mon)
You sell 20 rice to the pedlar at 5 mon the measure. (+1 monme 20 mon)
The house's word is worth less down the valley than it was: at the stall its name still buys, but the amounts are counted slowly. (+9 koku)
You sell 20 rice to the pedlar at 6 mon the measure. (+1 monme 40 mon)
You sell 20 rice to the pedlar at 6 mon the measure. (+1 monme 40 mon)
You sell 20 rice to the pedlar at 6 mon the measure. (+1 monme 40 mon)
You sell 20 rice to the pedlar at 6 mon the measure. (+1 monme 40 mon)
You sell 20 rice to the pedlar at 6 mon the measure. (+1 monme 40 mon)
You sell 20 rice to the pedlar at 6 mon the measure. (+1 monme 40 mon)
You sell 20 rice to the pedlar at 6 mon the measure. (+1 monme 40 mon)
You sell 20 rice to the pedlar at 6 mon the measure. (+1 monme 40 mon)
The house's word is worth less down the valley than it was: at the stall its name still buys, but the amounts are counted slowly. (+8 koku)
You sell 24 rice to the pedlar at 5 mon the measure. (+1 monme 40 mon)
You sell 20 rice to the pedlar at 5 mon the measure. (+1 monme 20 mon)
The valley lets the house be — greeted on the road, owed nothing, promised nothing. This year, being left alone is a kind of standing. (+9 koku)
You sell 24 rice to the pedlar at 5 mon the measure. (+1 monme 40 mon)
You sell 24 rice to the pedlar at 5 mon the measure. (+1 monme 40 mon)
The valley lets the house be — greeted on the road, owed nothing, promised nothing. This year, being left alone is a kind of standing. (+9 koku)
You sell 30 rice to the pedlar at 4 mon the measure. (+1 monme 40 mon)
You sell 30 rice to the pedlar at 4 mon the measure. (+1 monme 40 mon)
You sell 29 rice to the pedlar at 4 mon the measure. (+1 monme 36 mon)
You sell 30 rice to the pedlar at 4 mon the measure. (+1 monme 40 mon)
You sell 30 rice to the pedlar at 4 mon the measure. (+1 monme 40 mon)
You sell 30 rice to the pedlar at 4 mon the measure. (+1 monme 40 mon)
You sell 30 rice to the pedlar at 4 mon the measure. (+1 monme 40 mon)
You sell 30 rice to the pedlar at 4 mon the measure. (+1 monme 40 mon)
You sell 20 rice to the pedlar at 4 mon the measure. (+1 monme)
A second granary is raised board by board and the kura kept past this winter's need. The storehouse takes in more than it pays out at last. (U3 · Prosperous)
You sell 30 rice to the pedlar at 4 mon the measure. (+1 monme 40 mon)

Autumn thins to its last day, and the board comes out at an hour it does not usually keep. The whole house comes with it: the family by the book, the hands by the door, and you in the corner — counted as part of the room, and not asked into it.

Genemon reads the year against the house. The harvest in and weighed, the lease, the winter's wood — and then the one thing the house owes the land, that must be answered before the season is allowed to turn.
Nobody reads the sum aloud. It arrives the way weather arrives: the room goes still around it, a breath drawn and not let go, every eye on the book and none on one another.

Chiyo looks once, and briefly, at a screen that will not be in its room by spring. That is all that is shown of it, and none of it is explained. You are learning the house's true size by what it will let itself be seen without.
The book closes. The house has answered for the land; its autumn can end. You are still in the corner. Nobody tells you the season is over. It is.
The nengu is reckoned: the year measured against the house, the shortfall named plainly and let stand. No one at the board says the figure twice.
The valley lets the house be — greeted on the road, owed nothing, promised nothing. This year, being left alone is a kind of standing. (+8 koku)
You sell 24 rice to the pedlar at 5 mon the measure. (+1 monme 40 mon)
You sell 24 rice to the pedlar at 5 mon the measure. (+1 monme 40 mon)
Word goes down the valley ahead of the season's close: the gate is kept, the roofs hold, and the house's name is said plainly again, without the pause that used to follow it. (+9 koku)
You sell 24 rice to the pedlar at 5 mon the measure. (+1 monme 40 mon)

You sell 20 rice to the pedlar at 5 mon the measure. (+1 monme 20 mon)
Word goes down the valley ahead of the season's close: the gate is kept, the roofs hold, and the house's name is said plainly again, without the pause that used to follow it. (+8 koku)
You sell 24 rice to the pedlar at 5 mon the measure. (+1 monme 40 mon)
The road bends toward the gate again — a favor asked of the house, a cart that slows without needing anything. The valley has resumed expecting things of it. (+9 koku)
Repairs holding, reclamation bearing fruit, the barn full past need — surplus and order, written down. Let it be entered plainly: the estate stands. (U4 · Risen)
Let it be entered plainly, for whoever keeps these books hereafter: the estate stands. Every line above this one was the building of it.
You sell 30 rice to the pedlar at 4 mon the measure. (+1 monme 40 mon)
You sell 30 rice to the pedlar at 4 mon the measure. (+1 monme 40 mon)
You sell 23 rice to the pedlar at 4 mon the measure. (+1 monme 12 mon)
You sell 21 rice to the pedlar at 4 mon the measure. (+1 monme 4 mon)

Autumn thins to its last day, and the board comes out at an hour it does not usually keep. The whole house comes with it: the family by the book, the hands by the door, and you in the corner — counted as part of the room, and not asked into it.

Genemon reads the year against the house. The harvest in and weighed, the lease, the winter's wood — and then the one thing the house owes the land, that must be answered before the season is allowed to turn.
Nobody reads the sum aloud. It arrives the way weather arrives: the room goes still around it, a breath drawn and not let go, every eye on the book and none on one another.

Chiyo looks once, and briefly, at a screen that will not be in its room by spring. That is all that is shown of it, and none of it is explained. You are learning the house's true size by what it will let itself be seen without.
The book closes. The house has answered for the land; its autumn can end. You are still in the corner. Nobody tells you the season is over. It is.
The nengu is reckoned: the year measured against the house, the shortfall named plainly and let stand. No one at the board says the figure twice.
The road bends toward the gate again — a favor asked of the house, a cart that slows without needing anything. The valley has resumed expecting things of it. (+8 koku)
You sell 24 rice to the pedlar at 5 mon the measure. (+1 monme 40 mon)

The village has it, by way of O-Yae, that the house keeps a man the river left. Some say lucky, some say the other thing. She reports both and owns neither.
You sell 24 rice to the pedlar at 5 mon the measure. (+1 monme 40 mon)
You sell 23 rice to the pedlar at 5 mon the measure. (+1 monme 35 mon)

The guest house's skirts at rest: grey ice skins the paddy water, and only the winter rows — daikon under straw — still want hands. The mud is iron all morning and mud again by noon, and the new year will be paid for from here like the old one.

The outer court of the guest house, frozen hard enough that every footfall carries; the day's work is still set out here, breath showing over it. Genemon's window is lit early and late while the new year's book is opened — what is done out here goes into it.

Rain since first light. The yard's work waits under the eaves; the woodshed roof holds, this year.

They say the lowland wages are up again; two village boys have gone to try them. Their fields will be a pair of hands short at harvest, and everyone knows whose.

The river comes up brown with the thaw and the weir takes the worst of it. Matsuzō walks his bank twice a day and says nothing yet.
